source: A Quick Review of E-PCM for Engineering Entrance - Physics
subject: Physics

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A uniform elastic plank moves due to a constant force F distributed uniformly over the end face. The area of end face is S and Young's modulus of the material is E. What is average strain produced in the direction of the force?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: What is the change in density of water in a lake at a depth of 400 m below the surface? The density of water at the surface is 1030 kg/m³ and bulk modulus of water is 2 × 10⁹ N/m².

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A wire is elongated by 2 mm when a brick is suspended from it. When the brick is immersed in water, the wire contracts by 0.6 mm. What is the density of brick? (Density of water = 1000 kg/m³)
questions.explanation: ΔL₁ = 2 mm (in air), contraction in water = 0.6 mm. Buoyant force reduces extension. ρ = ρ_w × ΔL₁/(ΔL₁ - ΔL₂) = 1000 × 2/0.6 = 3333 kg/m³

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A metal block is experiencing an atmospheric pressure of 10⁵ N/m². When the same block is placed in a vacuum chamber, the fractional change in its volume (the bulk modulus of metal is 1.25 × 10¹¹ N/m²) is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Four wires of the same material are stretched by the same load. The dimensions of the wires are as given below. The one which has the maximum elongation is:
questions.explanation: ΔL = FL/(πr²Y). ΔL ∝ L/d². Comparing L/d² for each: (a) 1/1=1, (b) 2/4=0.5, (c) 0.5/0.25=2, (d) 3/9=0.33. Option C has maximum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A brass rod of length 2 m and cross-sectional area 2.0 cm² is attached end to end to a steel rod of length L and cross-sectional area 1.0 cm². The compound rod is subjected to equal and opposite pulls of magnitude 5 × 10⁴ N at its ends. If the elongations of the two rods are equal, the length of the steel rod (L) is (Y_brass = 1.0 × 10¹¹ N/m² and Y_steel = 2.0 × 10¹¹ N/m²)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An elevator cable is to have a maximum stress of 7 × 10⁷ N/m² to allow for appropriate safety factors. Its maximum upward acceleration is 1.5 m/s². If the cable has to support the total weight of 2000 kg of a loaded elevator, the area of cross-section of the cable should be:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A uniform steel bar of cross-sectional area A and length L is suspended so that it hangs vertically. The stress at the middle point of the bar is (ρ is the density of steel)
questions.explanation: Weight below midpoint = (L/2)Aρg. Stress = force/area = (L/2)Aρg/A = Lρg/2

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A cable that can support a load W is cut into two equal parts. The maximum load that can be supported by either part is:
questions.explanation: Breaking stress is independent of length. Since cross-section remains same, each part can support the same load W.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Young's modulus of a perfectly rigid body is:
questions.optionD: may have any finite non-zero value
questions.explanation: For a rigid body, ΔL = 0, so Y = FL/(AΔL) → ∞

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In practice, Poisson's ratio σ lies between:
questions.explanation: Practical value of Poisson's ratio lies between 0 and 0.5

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The length of a metal wire is l₁ when the tension in it is T₁ and is l₂ when the tension is T₂. The unstretched length of the wire is:
questions.explanation: l₁ = l₀ + T₁l₀/(AY), l₂ = l₀ + T₂l₀/(AY). Solving: l₀ = (l₁T₂ - l₂T₁)/(T₂ - T₁)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Young's modulus of brass and steel are respectively 10 × 10¹⁰ N/m² and 2 × 10¹⁰ N/m². A brass wire and a steel wire of the same length are extended by 1 mm under the same force; the radii of brass and steel wires are R_B and R_S respectively. Then:
questions.explanation: ΔL = FL/(πr²Y). For same ΔL: R²_B × Y_B = R²_S × Y_S. R²_B × 10 = R²_S × 2. R_S = √5 × R_B. Given the options, R_S = √2 × R_B when Y_B/Y_S = 2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The length of an elastic string is 'a' metre when the tension is 4 newton, and 'b' metre when the tension is 5 newton. The length in metres when the tension is 9 newton is:
questions.explanation: F ∝ elongation (ΔL). Let l₀ = unstretched length. a = l₀ + 4c, b = l₀ + 5c. So c = b - a, l₀ = 5a - 4b. At T=9: l = l₀ + 9c = 5a - 4b + 9(b-a) = 5b - 4a

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A stress of 10⁶ N/m² is required for breaking a material. If the density of the material is 3 × 10³ kg/m³, then what should be the length of the wire made of this material so that it breaks under its own weight:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A uniform pressure P is exerted on all sides of a solid cube at temperature t°C. By what amount should the temperature be raised in order to bring the volume back to that it had been before the pressure was applied? (Linear expansivity of the material of the cube is α and the bulk modulus of elasticity is β)
questions.explanation: ΔV/V = P/β. Also ΔV/V = 3αΔT. So ΔT = P/(3αβ)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A wire is suspended by one end. At the other end a weight Mg is attached. The wire extended by l. The ratio of mechanical energy stored in the stretched wire to the work done by Mg is:
questions.explanation: Mechanical energy U = (1/2)Mgl. Work done by Mg = Mgl. Ratio = 1/2

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A vessel of 1 × 10⁻³ m³ volume contains an oil. If a pressure of 1.2 × 10⁵ N/m² is applied on it then volume decreases by 0.3 × 10⁻⁶ m³. The bulk modulus of oil is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Which one of the following affects the elasticity of a substance?
questions.explanation: Temperature, hammering/annealing, and impurities all affect elasticity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A stretched rubber has:
questions.explanation: Stretching stores elastic potential energy in the rubber.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A ball falling in a lake of depth 200 m creates a decrease of 0.1% in its volume at the bottom. The bulk modulus of the material of that ball will be:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A bob of mass 10 kg is attached to a wire 0.3 m long. Its breaking stress is 4.8 × 10⁷ N/m². The area of cross-section of the wire is 10⁻⁶ m². What is the maximum angular velocity with which it can be rotated in a horizontal circle?
questions.explanation: T = stress × A = 4.8×10⁷×10⁻⁶ = 48 N. T = mω²r → 48 = 10×ω²×0.3 → ω = 4 rad/s

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: For a given material the Young's modulus is 2.4 times that of rigidity modulus. The Poisson's ratio is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A thick copper rope of density 1.5 × 10³ kg/m³ and Young's modulus 5 × 10⁶ N/m², 8 m in length is hung from the ceiling of a room. The increase in its length due to its own weight will be:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Two wires of same material and length but diameter in the ratio 1:3 are stretched by the same force. The potential energy per unit volume for the two wires when stretched will be in the ratio:
questions.explanation: Energy density = stress²/(2Y) = (F/A)²/(2Y). Ratio = (A₂/A₁)² = (d₂/d₁)⁴ ... = (3)⁴ : 1 = 81:1

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A copper rod 2 m long is stretched by 1 mm. Y = 1.2 × 10¹¹ N/m² is given. Then the energy stored per unit volume will be:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The breaking stress of a wire depends upon:
questions.explanation: Breaking stress is a material property, independent of dimensions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Young's modulus of a wire of length (L) and radius (r) is Y. If the length is reduced to L/2 and radius r/2, then its Young's modulus will be:
questions.explanation: Young's modulus is a material property, independent of dimensions of the specimen.
